name: Graph Theory & the Handshake Lemma
whatIsIt: A GRAPH is a set of dots (VERTICES) joined by lines (EDGES) — a map of connections. The DEGREE of a vertex is how many edges touch it. The HANDSHAKE LEMMA says the sum of ALL the degrees equals TWICE the number of edges (because every edge has two ends). A neat consequence: the number of ODD-degree vertices is always EVEN — so at a party, the count of people who shook an odd number of hands is even.
whyNeeded: Networks, routes, friendships, and scheduling are all graphs. The handshake lemma is the first powerful counting fact about them and appears throughout Olympiad combinatorics.
realLifeUses: Counting connections in a friendship or social network
Planning routes between cities (roads as edges)
Checking a network wiring is consistent

vocabulary.meaning: A dot in the graph (a person, city, or item).
A line joining two vertices (a connection).
The number of edges meeting at a vertex.

story.title: The Handshakes
story.text: At a party Riya wonders, 'If everyone counts their handshakes and we add them up, what do we get?' Uncle says, 'Each handshake involves TWO people, so it's counted twice — the total of everyone's counts is 2 × the number of handshakes.' With 5 handshakes, the degrees add to 10. 'And the number of people who shook an ODD number of hands is always even,' he adds. Riya smiles: 'Every edge has two ends — that's the whole secret.'
story.extractedProblem: If a graph has 5 edges, what is the sum of all the vertex degrees?
story.answerInStory: 10 — the handshake lemma gives sum of degrees = 2 × edges = 2 × 5.

visual.caption: The handshake lemma: adding up every vertex's degree gives twice the number of edges, because each edge is counted at both of its ends.

standardMethod.summary: Model the situation as vertices and edges. Add up the degrees; this equals 2 × (number of edges). To find edges from degrees, halve the degree sum. Use that odd-degree vertices come in an even count.
steps: The degree of a vertex = edges meeting it.
Sum of all degrees = 2 × number of edges.
Number of edges = (sum of degrees) ÷ 2; odd-degree vertices are even in count.

workedExamples.problem: A graph has 5 edges. What is the sum of all the vertex degrees?
Four vertices each have degree 3. How many edges are there?

commonMistakes.mistake: Setting the sum of degrees equal to the number of edges.
commonMistakes.fix: Sum of degrees = 2 × edges — each edge is counted at BOTH ends.

commonMistakes.mistake: Thinking there can be an odd number of odd-degree vertices.
commonMistakes.fix: The number of odd-degree vertices is always EVEN (it follows from the handshake lemma).

The handshake lemma says degree sum equals…

teachBackPrompt: Use handshakes with a grown-up. Explain that if everyone counts their own handshakes and you add them up, you get twice the number of handshakes — because each handshake has two people. So degree sum = 2 × edges, and odd-degree vertices come in an even count.

revisionCard.summary: Graph = vertices + edges. Degree = edges at a vertex. Handshake lemma: sum of degrees = 2 × edges (each edge counted twice). So edges = (degree sum) ÷ 2, and the number of odd-degree vertices is always even.

rememberIt.hook: Sum of degrees = 2 × edges (every edge has two ends)
rememberIt.unpack: Edges = degree sum ÷ 2. Odd-degree vertices always come in an even count.

studentQuestions.q: Why must odd-degree vertices be even in number?
studentQuestions.a: The total degree is even (it's 2 × edges). Even-degree vertices add an even amount, so the odd-degree ones must also total an even amount — meaning there's an even number of them.
One where every pair of vertices is joined. With n vertices it has n(n−1)/2 edges and every vertex has degree n−1.

formulas.name: Handshake lemma
formulas.remember: each edge counted at both ends

formulas.name: Complete graph edges
formulas.whenToUse: Counting edges when all pairs connect.
